summary: The doc_build_status_check job fails its conditional check because the workflow step compares literal strings ("failure" and "skipped") instead of using GitHub Actions context variables to check actual job statuses.
root_cause: The bash conditional uses literal string comparisons instead of GitHub Actions context variables. The condition `[[ "failure" == "success" || "failure" == "skipped" ]]` evaluates to FALSE (since the literal string "failure" matches neither), causing the job to exit with code 1.

evidence.quote:
if [[ "failure" == "success" || "failure" == "skipped" ]] && \
   [[ "skipped" == "success" || "skipped" == "skipped" ]]; then
  echo "OK"
else
  exit 1
fi
evidence.reason: The condition compares literal strings instead of job status variables. "failure" will never equal "success" or "skipped", so the first part is FALSE. The second part is TRUE ("skipped" == "skipped"), and FALSE && TRUE = FALSE, triggering the exit 1 branch.
Confirms the job exited with code 1, which is the result of the else branch in the conditional.
This job name indicates it is meant to check documentation build status, suggesting it should be reading actual job status values, not comparing hardcoded strings.

suggested_fix: Replace the literal string comparisons with GitHub Actions context variables (e.g., `${{ needs.previous_job_name.result }}`) that reference the actual status of dependent jobs in the workflow.
